questions.question: What is the time window for IV thrombolysis (alteplase) in acute ischemic stroke?
options: A. ≤2 hours
B. ≤3 hours
C. ≤4.5 hours
D. ≤6 hours
questions.rationale: IV tPA (alteplase) is approved for acute ischemic stroke within 4.5 hours of symptom onset, as reaffirmed by the 2025 AHA guidelines.

questions.question: What is the correct dose and administration of IV tPA for stroke?
options: A. 0.3 mg/kg, max 30 mg, all as a bolus
B. 0.9 mg/kg, max 90 mg, 10% bolus over 1 min then remainder over 60 min
C. 1.0 mg/kg, max 100 mg, infused over 30 min
D. 0.5 mg/kg, max 50 mg, 50% bolus then 50% over 60 min
questions.rationale: The stroke tPA dose is 0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg), with 10% given as a bolus over 1 minute and the remaining 90% infused over 60 minutes.

questions.question: What blood pressure must be achieved BEFORE administering tPA for stroke?
questions.rationale: Blood pressure must be <185/110 mmHg before tPA is initiated. Use IV labetalol 10–20 mg or nicardipine infusion to achieve this target.

questions.question: What blood pressure target must be maintained DURING and AFTER tPA infusion?
options: B. <180/105 mmHg for at least 24 hours
C. <140/90 mmHg for 48 hours
D. <200/110 mmHg for 12 hours
questions.rationale: During and for at least 24 hours after tPA, blood pressure must be maintained <180/105 mmHg, with approximately 15% reduction in the first 24 hours.

questions.question: What is the most important stroke mimic to rule out before initiating treatment?
options: A. Bell's palsy
B. Complex partial seizure
C. Hypoglycemia
D. Migraine with aura
questions.rationale: Hypoglycemia is the most critical and immediately treatable stroke mimic. Always check blood glucose in every suspected stroke presentation.

questions.question: What is the time window for mechanical thrombectomy in large-vessel occlusion with standard imaging?
options: A. ≤3 hours
B. ≤6 hours
C. ≤12 hours
D. ≤24 hours
questions.rationale: The standard time window for mechanical thrombectomy is within 6 hours of symptom onset using conventional imaging. Advanced perfusion imaging can extend this to 24 hours in selected patients.

questions.question: What blood pressure threshold is allowed for a non-tPA stroke candidate in the first 24 hours?
options: C. Up to 220/120 mmHg if no hemorrhage or cardiac ischemia
D. No permissive hypertension is allowed
questions.rationale: Permissive hypertension up to 220/120 mmHg is allowed in the first 24 hours for non-tPA candidates, provided there is no evidence of intracranial hemorrhage, cardiac ischemia, or aortic dissection.

questions.question: What imaging must be completed BEFORE administering tPA for stroke?
options: A. MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging
B. CT angiography of the head and neck
C. Non-contrast CT of the head
D. CT perfusion scan
questions.rationale: A non-contrast CT of the head is mandatory before tPA to exclude intracranial hemorrhage. This is the single most critical imaging step in stroke.

questions.question: How does Bell's palsy differ from central facial droop seen in stroke?
options: A. Bell's palsy involves the upper face; stroke spares it
B. Bell's palsy spares the forehead; stroke involves it
C. Bell's palsy affects both sides; stroke is unilateral
D. Bell's palsy affects the forehead and lower face; stroke affects only the upper face
questions.rationale: Bell's palsy (peripheral facial nerve) affects the ENTIRE ipsilateral face including the forehead. Central facial droop (stroke) spares the forehead because the upper face receives bilateral cortical innervation.

questions.question: Should tPA be withheld while evaluating a patient for mechanical thrombectomy?
options: A. Yes — always wait for thrombectomy evaluation first
B. No — give tPA immediately if criteria are met; proceed to thrombectomy without delay
C. Yes — tPA and thrombectomy are mutually exclusive
D. Only if thrombectomy is expected within 30 minutes
questions.rationale: tPA should be given immediately if the patient meets criteria. Patients eligible for both tPA and thrombectomy receive tPA first and proceed to thrombectomy without delay — the two treatments are complementary.

questions.question: What is the approximate rate of neuronal loss in untreated large-vessel ischemic stroke?
options: A. 100,000 neurons per minute
B. 500,000 neurons per minute
C. 1.9 million neurons per minute
D. 5 million neurons per minute
questions.rationale: Approximately 1.9 million neurons are lost per minute of untreated large-vessel ischemic stroke, underscoring the urgency of 'time is brain.'

questions.question: Which drug lowers blood pressure before tPA in stroke?
options: A. IV nitroglycerin
B. IV labetalol 10–20 mg over 1–2 minutes or nicardipine infusion
C. IV furosemide 40 mg
D. PO amlodipine 10 mg
questions.rationale: IV labetalol 10–20 mg over 1–2 minutes (repeat once) or nicardipine infusion (5 mg/hr, titrate up by 2.5 mg/hr q5–15 min, max 15 mg/hr) are used to lower BP before tPA.

questions.question: A stroke patient has a glucose of 240 mg/dL. The resident says 'hyperglycemia doesn't matter in stroke.' What is the correct response?
options: A. The resident is correct — hyperglycemia has no effect on stroke outcomes.
B. The resident is wrong — glucose should be maintained at 70–180 mg/dL; both hypo- and hyperglycemia (>180 mg/dL) worsen stroke outcomes.
C. Only hypoglycemia matters — hyperglycemia is always harmless.
D. Glucose management is not part of acute stroke care.
questions.rationale: Glucose should be maintained at 70–180 mg/dL. Both hypoglycemia (<60 mg/dL) and severe hyperglycemia (>180 mg/dL) are associated with worse outcomes in acute ischemic stroke. Insulin should be used for glucose >180 mg/dL.

questions.question: What should be done with fever in an acute stroke patient?
options: A. Allow permissive hyperthermia up to 39°C
B. Treat aggressively with antipyretics and cooling — >38°C worsens ischemic brain injury
C. Only treat if temperature exceeds 40°C
D. Fever is beneficial in stroke and should not be treated
questions.rationale: Fever (>38°C) worsens ischemic brain injury and must be treated aggressively and immediately with antipyretics and active cooling measures.

questions.question: A patient with left MCA syndrome and symptom onset 14 hours ago has CTA showing M1 occlusion. CT perfusion shows a small infarction core with large salvageable penumbra. What is the most appropriate management?
options: A. No intervention possible — the 4.5-hour tPA and 6-hour thrombectomy windows have closed.
B. Mechanical thrombectomy — advanced perfusion imaging extends eligibility up to 24 hours in selected patients with salvageable penumbra (DAWN/DEFUSE-3 criteria).
C. Transfer to hospice — no treatment options exist beyond 6 hours.
D. Administer tPA regardless of the time window.
questions.rationale: CT perfusion showing salvageable penumbra with limited core extends the thrombectomy window to 24 hours per DAWN and DEFUSE-3 trials. This patient has LVO with favorable perfusion profile — thrombectomy is indicated.

questions.question: A patient has normal facial symmetry, no arm drift, but abnormal speech when repeating a phrase. How many items on the Cincinnati Prehospital Stroke Scale are abnormal, and what does this indicate?
options: A. Zero abnormal — no stroke concern
B. One abnormal — high probability of stroke; activate stroke response
C. Two abnormal — this is a TIA, not a stroke
D. Three abnormal — the scale is normal
questions.rationale: One of three items (speech) is abnormal. The Cincinnati scale has high positive predictive value when ANY one of the three items is abnormal — this patient has a high probability of stroke and requires immediate stroke activation.
